engineering.translation: First: your jurisdiction extends only to the action itself — the function you can refactor, the migration you can execute, the deploy you can run through the pipeline. The fruits of that action — the conversion lift, the launch reception, the dashboard reading the next morning — are never your jurisdiction, at any time. Second: do not regard yourself as the cause of those fruits. The system, the users, the market, the timing produce the outcome; you do not. To inwardly claim authorship of the result is to bind yourself as the agent of consequences you cannot control. Third: do not let this become a license for retreat. Hiding inside low-stakes refactors, comfortable rewrites no one asked for, or perpetual analysis that defers the merge — that is inaction disguised as caution, and it is rejected here just as firmly as the grasping for fruits. Right action, disowned authorship of the result, no flight into the safe-feeling tasks.
engineering.concrete_scenario: It is the night before a Postgres schema migration. The pull request on GitHub has been reviewed, tests pass under CI, the rollback plan sits in the runbook, observability for the new endpoint is wired through Datadog and Sentry. You have already done the work that is genuinely yours to do. Now the temptation arrives: the launch tweet might flop, the conversion metric might not move, a competitor's release might eclipse yours next morning. Your hand hesitates above `git push` — not because the code is wrong, but because the outcome feels uncertain. Or consider the inverse failure: across two recent weeks you have been rewriting an internal CLI nobody asked for, telling yourself the rewrite is more elegant than the customer-facing schema migration languishing on the queue. You congratulate yourself on the technical purity of the rewrite while drifting into a more comfortable form of inaction. Both behaviors are this verse. Your right is to the action — the merge, the deploy, the migration. The tweet's reception, the dashboard reading, your peer's reaction — those belong elsewhere.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses it to justify shipping without reading Datadog dashboards, ignoring Sentry error reports, or skipping post-launch instrumentation. Detachment from outcome is not detachment from feedback. Anyone who concludes from this verse that they should stop looking at metrics has misread it entirely.
engineering.counter_example: When the genuinely correct action is to NOT ship — to kill a project, decline a feature request, or hold a release because incident data is incomplete — this verse does not contradict that. Krishna rejects inaction used as cover for fear of consequences; he does not reject the disciplined no. Chapter 5 and the closing instruction at 18.66 carry that complement, where right-renunciation is named explicitly.
engineering.implication: Read your dashboards as honestly as you read your code reviews. Let outcomes inform future actions; do not let outcome-anxiety determine present ones.
engineering.quotable_line: Your right is to the deploy, not to the launch metrics.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: initial engineering layer drafted from triangulated source pack (holy-bhagavad-gita.org Mukundananda + gitasupersite.iitk.ac.in Sanskrit + bhagavad-gita.us IAST + Shankara/Ramanuja commentary summaries).

Refined engineering.translation to preserve the three distinct prohibitions of the second hemistich (jurisdiction / agency-disowning / rejection-of-inaction). Triggered by cross-persona signal in the LLM-judge run: sanskrit-scholar + indian-philosopher + distortion-test independently flagged compression of (1) right-to-action / (2) not-being-cause-of-fruits / (3) not-attached-to-inaction. v3.1 separates the three movements while preserving Shankara's agency-binding emphasis and Ramanuja's inaction-rejection emphasis.
